title: Commitment timeline & owners — Lyra Health Q4 go/no-go
Redlines: Exhibit D (Pricing Schedule) — fee lift mechanism clarification
preview:
From: Elena Price <elena.price@acmehealth.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.inference>
Cc: procurement@acmehealth.com
Date: Mon, 24 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Redlines: Exhibit D - Pricing Schedule and Fee Lift Mechanism

Chloe —

Attached is Acme's redline to Exhibit D (pricing schedule). The primary open items are:

1) Fee lift mechanism (Section 2(b)): Acme's finance team pushed back on a single, uncapped percentage step when monthly committed usage is exceeded. They're asking for either a gradual step-up (tiered) or an annual smoothing credit so spikes don't create a retrospective lift for one month that skews the whole year.

2) Caps & floors: We propose preserving the floor (minimum monthly fee) but ask for a hard annual cap on aggregate uplift so budget doesn't blow out.

3) Packaging mapping: The 'SKU mapping' note in the order schedule is ambiguous — does fee lift apply at the SKU level, product family, or consolidated account? We need explicit language for how usage is aggregated.

Please confirm receipt and whether Redwood can accept (i) tiered lift thresholds, (ii) an annual cap, and (iii) clarified SKU aggregation. We prefer redline edits but have also attached a short rationale doc.

Regards,
Elena Price
Head of Platform Procurement
Acme Health

[Attachment: Exhibit D - Pricing Schedule (Acme redline).pdf]

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.inference>
To: Elena Price <elena.price@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>
Date: Mon, 24 Aug 2026 11:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Redlines: Exhibit D - Pricing Schedule and Fee Lift Mechanism

Elena —

Thanks, received and read. Quick notes while I pull the team together:

• We hear the concern on short-term spikes causing disproportionate uplift.
• We're open to a tiered approach but need to preserve the ability to protect against sustained over-usage that materially impacts capacity/costs.
• SKU aggregation: Redwood's standard is per "billing identity" (account + order schedule) but we can define product-family grouping if that better fits your internal chargebacks.

I've attached a short alternative language (Proposed language v1.docx) that (a) introduces a 3-tier lift (5%/12%/25%), (b) includes an annual smoothing credit if monthly lift exceeds X% of annual spend, and (c) ties aggregation to the OrderSchedule ID unless parties agree in writing to a different grouping.

Would you be available for a 30‑minute call Wednesday to walk through? If so we'll invite pricing + legal.

Best,
Chloe Martin
Senior Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

[Attachment: Proposed language v1.docx]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.inference>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.inference>
Date: Mon, 24 Aug 2026 13:55:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Acme redline — internal pricing/legal thoughts

Chloe / team —

Read through Acme's redline and Chloe's proposed language. High-level concerns from legal/pricing:

• Annual cap: reasonable to offer a hard cap on "aggregate annual uplift" but we need to define carve-outs (force majeure, negotiated temporary bursts, billing errors). Suggest: "Aggregate annual uplift shall not exceed 15% of the Annual Recurring Fee, excluding amounts attributable to documented system misuse or emergency bursts pre-approved by Redwood."

• Tiered lift: prefer tiered, but cap the maximum step so we have a predictable worst-case (e.g., maximum uplift of 25% in a single month and a maximum across year capped as above).

• SKU aggregation: require we define "Billing Unit" in the Order Schedule. Default = consolidated account, opt-out by mutual agreement. This prevents Acme from fragmenting consumption to avoid lift.

Next steps I propose:
1) Agree internal positions (Chloe/Ben/Logan) and send a consolidated counter with tracked redline.
2) Offer Acme the option for an "annual smoothing credit" (applied to the last quarter) if monthly lifts materially exceed expectation.

Marking up Chloe's doc and will send language suggestions by EOD.

-Marissa

(On Mon, Aug 24, 2026 at 11:02 AM Chloe Martin wrote:)
> Elena —
>
> Thanks, received and read. Quick notes while I pull the team together:
>
> • We hear the concern on short-term spikes causing disproportionate uplift.
> • We're open to a tiered approach but need to preserve the ability to protect against sustained over-usage that materially impacts capacity/costs.
>
> [Attachment: Proposed language v1.docx]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.inference>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.inference>
Date: Tue, 25 Aug 2026 09:10:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Acme redline — packaging / SKU mapping impact

Team —

Some practical examples to think about for the SKU aggregation question (these are hypothetical):

• Example A — Single product family: Acme runs "CareAssist" across 3 hospitals under one OrderSchedule. If aggregation is per OrderSchedule, the lift triggers when total CareAssist tokens exceed thresholds across hospitals (preferred for Redwood).

• Example B — Fragmented SKUs: If Acme defines separate SKUs per hospital, they could push usage to different lines to avoid per-line thresholds; we protect against this by making the default aggregation consolidated by Billing Identity.

Proposal: default = consolidated account (OrderSchedule ID). If Acme requests different grouping, require written approval + define a migration window (60 days) to reconcile historic usage. Also recommend a minimum reporting granularity clause (monthly token rolls) so both sides can see the behavior that caused lift.

Attaching a short CSV stub we can send to Acme showing the token aggregation by OrderSchedule for a sample month.

-Ben

[Attachment: OrderSchedule-attachment.csv]

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.inference>
To: Elena Price <elena.price@acmehealth.com>
Cc: procurement@acmehealth.com, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.inference>
Date: Wed, 26 Aug 2026 08:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Redlines: Exhibit D - Pricing Schedule and Fee Lift Mechanism (Consolidated counter)

Elena —

Appreciate the clear redline. Attached is Redwood's consolidated counter (Exhibit D - Proposed Redline.docx). Key points: 

1) Tiered lift: 3 tiers at 5% / 12% / 20% applied to the monthly invoice when the usage in a billing month exceeds the committed monthly token threshold by >10%, >30%, and >60% respectively.

2) Annual uplift cap: aggregate uplift in any 12‑month period shall not exceed 15% of the Annual Recurring Fee, excluding (a) documented misuse, (b) emergency bursts pre-approved by Customer, and (c) amounts resulting from post-billing corrections.

3) Aggregation: default Billing Unit = OrderSchedule ID (consolidated by default). Any alternative grouping must be mutually agreed in writing and subject to a 60-day reconciliation/transition period.

4) Annual smoothing credit: if total monthly lifts across the year exceed the annual cap threshold, Customer is eligible for a smoothing credit applied to the final invoice of the 12‑month period (details in paragraph 4.3).

We also included a clean example table in the counter showing a sample invoice calculation for a spike month vs. smoothed year. Please review the counter and let us know whether procurement + finance can accept these guardrails or prefer alternate percentages. If helpful, I'll set up a 30-minute call (propose Thu 9am PT).

DocuSign/next steps: we've prepared a DocuSign envelope (link below) for the updated Exhibit D if Acme is ready to accept. Otherwise we can continue markups.

Thanks,
Chloe Martin
Senior Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

[Attachment: Exhibit D - Proposed Redline.docx]
[Attachment: OrderSchedule-attachment.csv]

DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/placeholder/ACME-REDW-0824

From: Elena Price <elena.price@acmehealth.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.inference>
Cc: procurement@acmehealth.com
Date: Thu, 27 Aug 2026 17:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Redlines: Exhibit D - Pricing Schedule and Fee Lift Mechanism

Chloe —

Cc: procurement@acmehealth.com
Date: Mon, 24 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Redlines: Exhibit D - Pricing Schedule and Fee Lift Mechanism

Chloe —

Attached is Acme's redline to Exhibit D (pricing schedule). The primary open items are:

1) Fee lift mechanism (Section 2(b)): Acme's finance team pushed back on a single, uncapped percentage step when monthly committed usage is exceeded. They're asking for either a gradual step-up (tiered) or an annual smoothing credit so spikes don't create a retrospective lift for one month that skews the whole year.

2) Caps & floors: We propose preserving the floor (minimum monthly fee) but ask for a hard annual cap on aggregate uplift so budget doesn't blow out.

3) Packaging mapping: The 'SKU mapping' note in the order schedule is ambiguous — does fee lift apply at the SKU level, product family, or consolidated account? We need explicit language for how usage is aggregated.

Please confirm receipt and whether Redwood can accept (i) tiered lift thresholds, (ii) an annual cap, and (iii) clarified SKU aggregation. We prefer redline edits but have also attached a short rationale doc.

Regards,
Elena Price
Head of Platform Procurement
Acme Health

[Attachment: Exhibit D - Pricing Schedule (Acme redline).pdf]

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.inference>

Date: Mon, 24 Aug 2026 11:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Redlines: Exhibit D - Pricing Schedule and Fee Lift Mechanism

Elena —

Thanks, received and read. Quick notes while I pull the team together:

• We hear the concern on short-term spikes causing disproportionate uplift.
• We're open to a tiered approach but need to preserve the ability to protect against sustained over-usage that materially impacts capacity/costs.
• SKU aggregation: Redwood's standard is per "billing identity" (account + order schedule) but we can define product-family grouping if that better fits your internal chargebacks.

I've attached a short alternative language (Proposed language v1.docx) that (a) introduces a 3-tier lift (5%/12%/25%), (b) includes an annual smoothing credit if monthly lift exceeds X% of annual spend, and (c) ties aggregation to the OrderSchedule ID unless parties agree in writing to a different grouping.

Would you be available for a 30‑minute call Wednesday to walk through? If so we'll invite pricing + legal.

Best,
Chloe Martin
Senior Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

[Attachment: Proposed language v1.docx]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.inference>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.inference>
Date: Mon, 24 Aug 2026 13:55:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Acme redline — internal pricing/legal thoughts

Chloe / team —

Read through Acme's redline and Chloe's proposed language. High-level concerns from legal/pricing:

• Annual cap: reasonable to offer a hard cap on "aggregate annual uplift" but we need to define carve-outs (force majeure, negotiated temporary bursts, billing errors). Suggest: "Aggregate annual uplift shall not exceed 15% of the Annual Recurring Fee, excluding amounts attributable to documented system misuse or emergency bursts pre-approved by Redwood."

• Tiered lift: prefer tiered, but cap the maximum step so we have a predictable worst-case (e.g., maximum uplift of 25% in a single month and a maximum across year capped as above).

• SKU aggregation: require we define "Billing Unit" in the Order Schedule. Default = consolidated account, opt-out by mutual agreement. This prevents Acme from fragmenting consumption to avoid lift.

Next steps I propose:
1) Agree internal positions (Chloe/Ben/Logan) and send a consolidated counter with tracked redline.
2) Offer Acme the option for an "annual smoothing credit" (applied to the last quarter) if monthly lifts materially exceed expectation.

Marking up Chloe's doc and will send language suggestions by EOD.

-Marissa

Team —

Some practical examples to think about for the SKU aggregation question (these are hypothetical):

• Example A — Single product family: Acme runs "CareAssist" across 3 hospitals under one OrderSchedule. If aggregation is per OrderSchedule, the lift triggers when total CareAssist tokens exceed thresholds across hospitals (preferred for Redwood).

• Example B — Fragmented SKUs: If Acme defines separate SKUs per hospital, they could push usage to different lines to avoid per-line thresholds; we protect against this by making the default aggregation consolidated by Billing Identity.

Proposal: default = consolidated account (OrderSchedule ID). If Acme requests different grouping, require written approval + define a migration window (60 days) to reconcile historic usage. Also recommend a minimum reporting granularity clause (monthly token rolls) so both sides can see the behavior that caused lift.

Attaching a short CSV stub we can send to Acme showing the token aggregation by OrderSchedule for a sample month.

1) Tiered lift: 3 tiers at 5% / 12% / 20% applied to the monthly invoice when the usage in a billing month exceeds the committed monthly token threshold by >10%, >30%, and >60% respectively.

2) Annual uplift cap: aggregate uplift in any 12‑month period shall not exceed 15% of the Annual Recurring Fee, excluding (a) documented misuse, (b) emergency bursts pre-approved by Customer, and (c) amounts resulting from post-billing corrections.

3) Aggregation: default Billing Unit = OrderSchedule ID (consolidated by default). Any alternative grouping must be mutually agreed in writing and subject to a 60-day reconciliation/transition period.

4) Annual smoothing credit: if total monthly lifts across the year exceed the annual cap threshold, Customer is eligible for a smoothing credit applied to the final invoice of the 12‑month period (details in paragraph 4.3).

We also included a clean example table in the counter showing a sample invoice calculation for a spike month vs. smoothed year. Please review the counter and let us know whether procurement + finance can accept these guardrails or prefer alternate percentages. If helpful, I'll set up a 30-minute call (propose Thu 9am PT).

Hi Elena / team,

Thanks again for the time yesterday. Per our conversation I attached a concise commercial proposal for Mediscope's private VPC deployment. High-level summary below so procurement can triage quickly: 

- Platform fee: $95k/year, covers control plane, model catalog, and observability (tested at the pilot scale described).
- Node credits: pre-purchase 40 node-credits (each credit = 1 GPU-equivalent-month) at $2,400/credit; unused credits roll over for 6 months.
- SRE retainer: optional 1, 3, 5 day SLA windows — starts at $12k/month for Bronze (business-hours) up to $45k/month for Platinum (24x7).
- Migration credit: one-time $30k implementation credit if you commit to a 24-month term.

Attachments: privdeploy-pricing-proposal-v1.pdf, sow-draft-mediscp-revA.docx

Questions for procurement:
1) Is a single invoice to Mediscope OK (platform fee + node credits), or do you need separate invoices for licensing vs hosting?
2) Any procurement-specific PO/contracting constraints we should know before legal redlines (billing cadence, PO templates, escrow requirements)?

Happy to hop on a quick call to walk through. If you'd like a different anchoring (lower platform fee + higher per-node price), we can propose that as an alternate.

Best,
Kimberly Park
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference

Kimberly —

Appreciate the fast turnaround and the clear options. A few quick asks from our side as procurement evaluates:

- The platform fee looks high relative to our expected steady-state footprint; can you run an alternate with a lower annual platform fee (e.g., $55k) offset by higher node credit pricing so the TCO for year 1 is roughly equivalent?

- Legal: we will need an escrow / source-escrow clause (for on-prem deployments) and a limitation of liability cap aligned with our procurement policy. Can you confirm Redwood accepts a mutual cap tied to fees?

On Wed, Aug 26, 2026 at 10:34 AM Elena Martinez <elena@mediscope.com> wrote:
> Kimberly —
> 
> Appreciate the fast turnaround and the clear options. A few quick asks from our side as procurement evaluates: ...


From: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 27 Aug 2026 08:27:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Private deployment commercial — initial proposal

Kim / Marissa —

Drafting two clean commercial constructs below. I focused on modular concessions that legal can accept without changing core product terms.

Option A — Starter-friendly anchor (12m pilot)
- Platform fee: $55,000/year (billed annually for pilot)
- Node credits: $2,950/credit, 40 credits recommended (year 1 run-rate); credits roll over for 3 months for pilot customers
- Migration credit: $20,000 (applied to first invoice)
- Extension option: 12m extension at a 7% uplift on node credits if exercised within 90 days of pilot end
- SLA/SRE: Bronze = business-hours response (8x5) 8-hour response, up to 10 incident-hours/month; Gold = 4-hour response, 20 incident-hours/month; Platinum = 1-hour response, on-call + 24x7 coverage
- Legal/escrow: propose mutual limitation of liability capped at the greater of $250k or 6 months of paid fees; escrow clause limited to binary artifacts used for deployment (no daily sync).

Option B — Full commercial (2-year preferred)
- Platform fee: $95,000/year (billed annually)
- Node credits: $2,200/credit, 48 credits recommended (year 1 committed)
- Migration credit: $30,000 + 60 days free SRE (Gold) during cutover
- Term: 24 months preferred; 12-month with 10% premium on node pricing

title: Clarify billing for hybrid prompts & bursty traffic
preview:
From: Kristen Martinez <kristen@omnidata.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-18T15:03:00-07:00
Subject: Clarify billing for hybrid prompts & bursty traffic

Hi Logan,

Quick question on billing units — we're trying to finalize our internal cost model ahead of the commit call next week and need to understand how Redwood will count tokens and apply minimums for our workload. A few specifics: 

- We have a hybrid pattern: ~65% short API calls (<250 tokens roundtrip), ~35% long-context chains where we pass ~30k-token context (ingestion + lookup) but rely heavily on prefix KV-cache reuse across calls.
- ~12% of requests use streaming outputs (server pushes partial tokens), the rest are regular non-streamed responses.
- We see very bursty traffic: short spikes that hit ~10k RPS for 30–90s during big batch jobs, otherwise steady baseline.

Questions:
1) For long-context requests when a large prefix is reused from KV cache, do you bill the full prompt length every request or is there a reuse credit? If reuse credit exists, how is it calculated (percentage, absolute token discount, or bucketed)?
2) How do you handle streaming? Are increments billed as tokens emitted or as the total eventual response?
3) Do you apply per-request floors/minimums and, if so, at what granularity (per-call, per-minute, per-session)? Any rounding rules for partial tokens/subtokens?
4) For bursty periods, are short bursts amortized by smoothing windows or do we get charged per-request at the peak rate?

Can you send a simple sample invoice example for a month with 1,000,000 billed tokens under a hypothetical 12-month commit with an 18% discount (we're trying to reconcile math for procurement). I attached a short spec and traffic sample.

Thanks — Kristen
Kristen Martinez
Head of ML, OmniData
---
attachment: sample_app_spec.pdf


From: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
To: Kristen Martinez <kristen@omnidata.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-18T17:10:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Clarify billing for hybrid prompts & bursty traffic

Hi Kristen — thanks for the clear breakdown and the attachments. Looping in Vanessa and Ben to make sure we give you an exact billing example. A couple quick clarifiers while they prepare: 

- For the long-context flows, can you estimate the average fraction of the 30k-token context that is truly unique per request vs. perfectly reusable across calls? Even a coarse split (e.g., 90% cached / 10% new) helps.
- For bursts: are those 10k RPS spikes coming from a single client IP/tenant or multiple parallel workers?

I attached your traffic extract (omni_sample_traffic.csv) with my notes.

—Logan
Logan Wright
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

attachment: omni_sample_traffic.csv


From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>
To: Kristen Martinez <kristen@omnidata.com>
Cc: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-19T09:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Clarify billing for hybrid prompts & bursty traffic

Hi Kristen — happy to dig in. High level on discounts: for committed 12-month seats + token commit we can target 18% off list for the tier you described; exact effective discount depends on commit size and whether Dedicated capacity/backed SLA is requested. Ben, can you confirm the canonical policy on: prefix reuse credits, streaming billing point, per-RPC minimums, and burst smoothing windows?

For procurement: if OmniData signs for a $100k/mo commit we typically provide an invoice-friendly worked example. We'll aim to get that to you with a 1-month projection and an annualized number.

—Vanessa
Vanessa Ortiz
Senior Sales Manager, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
Cc: Kristen Martinez <kristen@omnidata.com>
Date: 2026-08-20T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Clarify billing for hybrid prompts & bursty traffic

Vanessa / Logan / Kristen — billing team here. Below is the canonical behavior (we've intentionally chosen names/terms that map to our platform behavior so the math is reproducible). Attaching a worked spreadsheet with line-item math.

Key rules (short):
1) Token accounting 
   - We bill the sum of input + output tokens (prompt + response) visible to the runtime at request evaluation time.
   - For KV/prefix cache reuse we apply a reuse credit when the runtime serves >256 tokens from the local KV cache without recomputation. Credit = 50% off those reused tokens (i.e., reused_token_charge = 0.5 * token_unit_price). This credit applies per-request and is visible on the token detail line.

2) Streaming 
   - We bill streaming as tokens emitted. Each token the client receives is billed in the same fashion as if it were returned in a single non-streamed response. There is no separate streaming surcharge.

3) Minimums & rounding 
   - Per-RPC floor: 6 tokens (we bill at least 6 tokens per request). This floor is to prevent microbilling noise on high-RT, very-short calls.
   - Minute-level smoothing: for bursty bursts, we apply a 15-minute smoothing window. If your traffic exceeds sustained baseline within the 15-minute window, we amortize the excess across that window rather than charging instantaneous peak for every request. In practice this reduces peak charge volatility for short spikes (30–90s), but sustained >15-minute bursts are charged at measured usage.
   - Subtoken rounding: we bill at subtoken precision up to 1 decimal (e.g., 1.3 subtokens -> rounded to 1.3); invoice totals are summed and then final rounding to the nearest whole token occurs at the invoice line-item level.

4) Pilot exceptions & overrides
   - For pilot/POCs we can temporarily waive the per-RPC floor or apply an alternate smoothing window (e.g., 60-minute amortization) — done case-by-case and must be requested in writing.

Worked example (simplified) — source: your sample traffic + 1,000,000 nominal tokens
   - Baseline token count (raw sum input+output): 1,000,000 tokens
   - Estimated KV-reused tokens (we used your 90% reuse estimate for the 30k-context set): 120,000 tokens are classified as reused (example). Reuse credit reduces billed units for those tokens by 50% -> billed equivalent = 120,000 * 0.5 = 60,000 tokens.
   - Per-RPC floors: across 220k RPCs in month, 18k RPCs hit the 6-token floor which increases billed tokens by +72,000 tokens (18k * (6 - actual_avg_for_those_calls)).
   - Streaming tokens counted as emitted: +12,000 tokens

   Effective billed total (after adjustments) = 1,000,000 - 120,000 + 60,000 + 72,000 + 12,000 = 1,024,000 tokens billed

Pricing & discount (example)
   - List price per 1k tokens: $0.30 (example bucket) -> gross = $307.2 (1,024 * 0.3) => $307.20 (for 1,024k tokens, see spreadsheet for decimal handling)
   - Commit discount (18%) => net = $252.00

Notes: the important levers here are the reuse credit and the 15-minute smoothing window — both materially reduce the effective cost for workloads with high KV reuse and short bursts. Both are configurable at contract time (we can negotiate a higher reuse credit for larger commits).

I've attached the billing_examples_omnidata.xlsx with line-by-line calculations, and a sample invoice PDF for the projected month. If you'd like we can also produce a CSV export formatted for your internal FinOps ingestion.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Pricing & Billing, Redwood Inference
---
attachment: billing_examples_omnidata.xlsx


From: Kristen Martinez <kristen@omnidata.com>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-24T09:11:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Clarify billing for hybrid prompts & bursty traffic

Thanks — that helps a lot. The reuse credit + 15-minute smoothing gets us very close to our internal model. Two asks before we sign: 

1) Can you reflect the 18% committed discount and the reuse credit in a revised PO/invoice stub? (I've attached our PO.)
2) Please send the DocuSign link for the master services agreement and the SOW referencing the billing profile.

If you can get those two items over by EOD Friday we can route the PO to procurement and close the deal.

Best,
Kristen
Kristen Martinez
Head of ML, OmniData

title: SDK streaming disconnects after long prompt
preview:
From: Maria Gomez <maria@velvethost.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-19T09:14:00-07:00
Subject: SDK streaming disconnects after long prompt

Hey Ben,

Thanks again for the demo this morning — Samir and I were able to exercise the streaming SDK during a long-document summarization flow. We hit an issue when the combined prompt + context exceeded ~2.5k tokens: the client shows a streaming disconnect and the server returns a 502 briefly. Re-attaching minimal repro logs and a short video clip.

Questions: is there a known limit on streaming/session length? Is there a recommended client-side retry pattern we should use to avoid partial outputs? We're evaluating Redwood for a critical feature so any guidance helps.

Attachments: velvet_demo_logs.zip (200KB), screen-clip.mp4 (referenced)

—Maria
Head of Product, VelvetHost


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Maria Gomez <maria@velvethost.com>

Hi Maria — really appreciate the quick repro and video. I'm sorry you ran into that during the demo.

Short answer: we don't have a hard client-side token cap, but we do have operational safeguards at the routing/edge and within the runtime that can abort or retry when memory pressure or unexpected kernel failure occurs. That said, a 502 is not expected for a normal streaming session.

From: Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-19T12:25:00-07:00
Subject: Re: SDK streaming disconnects after long prompt — internal triage

Ben — thanks for the ping and logs. Quick read: the server-side traceback in velvet_demo_logs.zip points to an OOM-triggered bailout inside the attention kernel when the prefix + generation window hits a particular alignment (long-ish prefix + medium generation). We saw similar failure modes earlier this year but they were mitigated by a runtime patch that tightened buffer checks; looks like this example exercises a rarer alignment.

Immediate suggestions for the call:
- Ask Maria to reproduce with the SDK using the --no-prefetch flag to rule out client prefetch/KV cache growth as a trigger.
- Try trimming user prompt prefix to see if crash correlates with prefix length vs generation length.
- If we can reproduce, we'll run with debug kernels enabled and capture a detailed core/log snapshot.

I'll stand by for the 10am PT repro. If VelvetHost can share a sanitized request payload ahead of time, that will speed things up (we can sign an NDA for that sample if needed).

—Logan
Principal Engineer, Serving Runtime

To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Patel <samir@velvethost.com>
Date: 2026-08-20T10:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: SDK streaming disconnects after long prompt — demo repro call

Thanks team — on the call now and just uploaded the sanitized sample to the Drive link. The file is named repro_payload_sanitized.json. We're ready for a live run.

—Maria


From: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-21T16:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: SDK streaming disconnects after long prompt — post-triage notes

Quick sync notes from the repro today (internal):
- We reproduced the disconnect with the sanitized payload. Root cause: an off-by-one in buffer sizing for pooled attention buffers when prefix lengths align on a 512-token boundary with a mid-sized generation window. This leads to a transient write past end-of-buffer and the runtime triggers a controlled 502 abort.
- Short-term mitigation (deployed as a hotfix to the staging cluster during call): runtime now pads pooled buffers by +1 allocation for the affected kernels. This reduces throughput by ~0.6% but prevents the crash. Patch id: RT-HF-2026-0819.
- Recommended SDK guidance for customers until official release: set --no-prefetch or limit prefix to <2300 tokens when doing long prompt + generation mixes; enable client-side single-shot retry with idempotency token to resume safely. We'll document this in the KB article.
- Next steps: create an internal RFC to make padding permanent with a perf-cost analysis, and include a test case in the CI repro matrix for long-prefix alignments. Logan to own RFC; Marissa to draft customer-facing KB; Ben to update HubSpot deal notes.

Attachments included for the team: repro_steps.txt, screenshot-rt-disconnect.png

—Sean
Staff Sales Engineer (Operations Liaison)

title: Audit scope, retention tradeoffs, and MVP rollout — follow-up
preview:
From: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>
To: Harper Lane <harper@tradelane.com>, Alex Soto <alex.soto@peakledger.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Audit scope, retention tradeoffs, and MVP rollout — follow-up

Hi Harper / Alex —

Thanks again for the call earlier — appreciate the clarity on the compliance bar you're working toward. Quick recap so we have shared expectations and a proposed path to an initial acceptance (MVP) that keeps your go‑live timeline intact.

Pain points you called out:
- Audit log retention: canonical logs for control evidence, and the need for an exportable stream into your SIEM (Splunk)
- Data retention windows: different retention for tokens vs. full request payloads; legal + product want a 90d default, security asking about 365d for transaction traces
- DPA & data residency: you need EU residency for European customer traffic and a DPA addendum with specific subprocessor listing
- Pen test: coordinated window and proof of remediation traceability
- SOC 2 evidence: a short artifact set (config screenshots, encrypted transport proof, policy excerpts) for your audit team to review

Proposed acceptance (MVP) criteria we discussed:
1) Audit exports available to your SIEM (near‑real time forwarding or S3 dumps)
2) Retention toggle options exposed: 30/90/365 days, with documented tradeoffs and cost delta
3) DPA addendum draft shared and signed (DocuSign link)
4) Pen test window scheduled + an evidence manifest to show remediation tracking
5) SOC2 snapshot (focused set) uploaded to your review folder

Immediate next steps I propose (with owners & rough timing):
- Logan: share the draft DPA and MVP acceptance doc by EOD Wed (18 Aug) — attachment: gap-analysis-template.xlsx
- Neha: provide SOC 2 snapshot + short note on encryption / key management and audit log format by Thu morning
- Harper / Alex: confirm preferred retention tier (30/90/365) and give list of SIEM ingest point (Splunk HEC or S3) by Fri
- Ben: coordinate pen test window options (2‑week window) and remediation process details (next week)

Attachments: gap-analysis-template.xlsx, mvp-acceptance-examples.pdf (examples of acceptance criteria we've used with similar banks).

If that matches what you heard, I’ll create a short acceptance doc and a shared Drive folder for evidence where you can pull artifacts as needed.

Thanks — talk soon.

—
Logan Wright
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
logan.wright@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555‑0189


From: Harper Lane <harper@tradelane.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 09:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Audit scope, retention tradeoffs, and MVP rollout — follow-up

Logan — thanks for the tidy recap. Couple clarifications from our side so we’re aligned: 

1) Retention: legal will likely sign off on 90d for most workloads, but we need 365d for settlement traces — can you confirm whether Redwood can keep encrypted transaction payloads for 365d while exposing a tokenized view at 90d?
2) SOC2 snapshot: the audit team wants TLS config screenshots, a copy of the retention policy, and a mapping of where audit logs are stored (format + TTL). Is that the manifest Neha will upload?
3) SIEM: we prefer Splunk HEC ingest; if you can provide either a direct HEC endpoint or daily S3 exports in NDJSON format that’ll work.

Also please include an estimate for the time-to-provide SOC2 artifacts from the moment we sign the DPA. We are targeting a pilot kickoff in mid‑September, so timeline is tight.

I’ll loop in procurement once DPA draft is in place.

—Harper
Harper Lane | Head of Product Security, TradeLane Capital
harper@tradelane.com | +1 212‑555‑0147

- We can support dual retention (tokenized summary at 90d, encrypted full payload at 365d) using our tiered KV store + cold S3 tiering. There is a small restore latency (~45m) for cold retrievals; call that out in the acceptance doc.
- Pen test: engineering prefers a 2‑week testing window; remediation tracking will be via Jira (we’ll provide a report with fix commit SHAs).
- SOC2 snapshot: Neha can compile the artifacts; estimate 48 hours once we have signed DPA & access to the Red/Green account mapping.

Ben
Ben Carter | Sales Engineering, Redwood Inference

From: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>
To: Harper Lane <harper@tradelane.com>, Alex Soto <alex.soto@peakledger.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Audit scope, retention tradeoffs, and MVP rollout — follow-up

Hi Harper / Alex —

Thanks again for the call earlier — appreciate the clarity on the compliance bar you're working toward. Quick recap so we have shared expectations and a proposed path to an initial acceptance (MVP) that keeps your go‑live timeline intact.

Pain points you called out:
- Audit log retention: canonical logs for control evidence, and the need for an exportable stream into your SIEM (Splunk)
- Data retention windows: different retention for tokens vs. full request payloads; legal + product want a 90d default, security asking about 365d for transaction traces
- DPA & data residency: you need EU residency for European customer traffic and a DPA addendum with specific subprocessor listing
- Pen test: coordinated window and proof of remediation traceability
- SOC 2 evidence: a short artifact set (config screenshots, encrypted transport proof, policy excerpts) for your audit team to review

Proposed acceptance (MVP) criteria we discussed:
1) Audit exports available to your SIEM (near‑real time forwarding or S3 dumps)
2) Retention toggle options exposed: 30/90/365 days, with documented tradeoffs and cost delta
3) DPA addendum draft shared and signed (DocuSign link)
4) Pen test window scheduled + an evidence manifest to show remediation tracking
5) SOC2 snapshot (focused set) uploaded to your review folder

Immediate next steps I propose (with owners & rough timing):
- Logan: share the draft DPA and MVP acceptance doc by EOD Wed (18 Aug) — attachment: gap-analysis-template.xlsx
- Neha: provide SOC 2 snapshot + short note on encryption / key management and audit log format by Thu morning
- Harper / Alex: confirm preferred retention tier (30/90/365) and give list of SIEM ingest point (Splunk HEC or S3) by Fri
- Ben: coordinate pen test window options (2‑week window) and remediation process details (next week)

Attachments: gap-analysis-template.xlsx, mvp-acceptance-examples.pdf (examples of acceptance criteria we've used with similar banks).

If that matches what you heard, I’ll create a short acceptance doc and a shared Drive folder for evidence where you can pull artifacts as needed.

Thanks — talk soon.

—
Logan Wright
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
logan.wright@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555‑0189

title: Partner onboarding: split claims, prospect ownership & guardrails
preview:
From: Alyssa Chen <alyssa@bridgersys.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 14 Aug 2026 09:12:34 -0700
Subject: Partner onboarding: pilot intro + split/registration questions

Hi Logan —

Thanks again for the quick demo earlier this week. Bridger is excited to move the pilot forward (targeting a September kickoff). Before we formalize, legal asked for a few clarifications so our revops folks can wire the prospect registration workflow into our CRM: 

Quick questions:
- What counts as a valid lead registration (form submit vs. demo intro vs. closed opportunity)?
- Do you do a first-to-register ownership rule or first-signed-opportunity?
- Is there a lookback/claim window for historical outreach (e.g., we have prior conversations with Acme Corp)?
- How do you handle situations where both Bridger and a Redwood AE engage the same account (anti-surfing protections)?
- What are the standard revenue-split and payment cadence for introductions vs. integrations?

If you have a short one-pager or partner playbook we can review, that would help speed legal sign-off. Attached our proposal draft for reference.

Thanks,
Alyssa
Head of Partnerships | Bridger Systems
[Attachment: Bridger-Proposal-v2.pdf (pdf)]


From: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
To: Alyssa Chen <alyssa@bridgersys.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 14 Aug 2026 11:03:11 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner onboarding: pilot intro + split/registration questions

Alyssa —

Appreciate the clear list. Below is a pragmatic set of guardrails we’ve been operating with that should map cleanly to your revops flow. Happy to iterate but this should unblock the pilot contract.

Partner registration and ownership
- Valid registration = partner registration form submission OR a documented demo intro that results in a logged HubSpot lead (we’ll accept either as provenance).
- Ownership rule = first-to-register wins. If multiple submissions exist, the earliest timestamped registration gets priority.
- Nurture and lookback windows = 90-day primary nurture from registration; 12-month claim window for prior contacts if partner provides CRM records showing prior engagement.

Anti-surfing / protection
- If a Redwood AE is already engaged (defined as active opportunity with a Redwood rep owner in our CRM), Redwood maintains the account for that open opportunity.
- If both Bridger and Redwood submit close-in-time registrations without an open opportunity, we mark it as a contested lead and trigger a quick review (30-day arbitration window).

Commission structure (pilot baseline)
- Intro-only (referral that results in paid revenue): 15% revenue share for first 12 months of subscription revenue.
- Technical integration (partner-led integration validated by Redwood): 20% for the first 12 months.
- Renewal cliff: commissions paid only on net new revenue; renewals beyond year one default to a 5% referral credit unless we co-sell.
- Clawback: refund/chargeback protection on cancellations within 60 days; see finance cadence below.

Operational items
- Payment cadence: payouts to partner are net-45 after Redwood receives payment from customer. Finance will require a partner W-9 / invoice cadence.
- Tracking: please register prospects in the partner portal: https://portal.redwood.com/partners/register?ref=bridger (this creates the HubSpot record automatically).

Next steps
1) I’ll circulate a short playbook and standard SOW template (draft attached).
2) If that looks good, legal can prepare a simple referral addendum + SOW that we can run through DocuSign.

Attaching the partner playbook for quick review.

—Logan
Head of Partnerships | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: partner-playbook-v1.pdf (pdf)]


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>, Alyssa Chen <alyssa@bridgersys.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 08:45:02 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner onboarding: pilot intro + split/registration questions

Looping in a couple of items from legal and finance so we don’t have surprises in the SOW: 

Legal notes (need to land in addendum)
- Clawback language: 60-day pro-rata refund window; for multi-year prepayments we require a defined waterfall for partner repayment (we’ll provide draft language).
- Evidence: partners must retain demonstrable provenance (form timestamps, signed intro emails) to assert ownership; we will accept CSV exports from partner CRM as supporting evidence.

Finance notes
- Payment schedule: net-45 after customer invoice is paid to Redwood. Commission remittance will be quarterly for pilots under $50k to reduce admin overhead.
- KYC/invoicing: Bridger needs to provide billing details (legal entity name, tax ID or W-9 equivalent).

Ops / tracking
- We created a stub HubSpot record for this flow: HUBS-2048123 — please use that as the canonical place while we wire partner portal integration.

I’ve attached a draft of our clawback terms so legal can comment inline. If everyone is aligned, I’ll ask contracts to spin a short referral addendum + pilot SOW and generate a DocuSign packet.

Thanks,
Marissa
Senior Partner Ops | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood-clawback-terms-draft.docx (docx)]


From: Alyssa Chen <alyssa@bridgersys.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 16:05:10 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner onboarding: pilot intro + split/registration questions

Thanks Logan and Marissa — this all looks straightforward and close to what Bridger expects. A few quick confirmations before we sign: 

- Bridger will use the partner portal registration as primary provenance and export our CRM thread history for disputed accounts.
- We accept the pilot baseline splits (15% intro / 20% integration) and the 60-day clawback window with pro-rata repayment.
- We’ll submit our W-9 and billing details by end of day Thursday.

Can Redwood send the short SOW + referral addendum via DocuSign? Alyssa (legal) will review but this should be quick. If you can include the HubSpot record id (HUBS-2048123) in the SOW that would be helpful.

Subject: Partner onboarding: pilot intro + split/registration questions

Hi Logan —

Thanks again for the quick demo earlier this week. Bridger is excited to move the pilot forward (targeting a September kickoff). Before we formalize, legal asked for a few clarifications so our revops folks can wire the prospect registration workflow into our CRM: 

Quick questions:
- What counts as a valid lead registration (form submit vs. demo intro vs. closed opportunity)?
- Do you do a first-to-register ownership rule or first-signed-opportunity?
- Is there a lookback/claim window for historical outreach (e.g., we have prior conversations with Acme Corp)?
- How do you handle situations where both Bridger and a Redwood AE engage the same account (anti-surfing protections)?
- What are the standard revenue-split and payment cadence for introductions vs. integrations?

If you have a short one-pager or partner playbook we can review, that would help speed legal sign-off. Attached our proposal draft for reference.

Thanks,
Alyssa
Head of Partnerships | Bridger Systems
[Attachment: Bridger-Proposal-v2.pdf (pdf)]


From: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
To: Alyssa Chen <alyssa@bridgersys.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 14 Aug 2026 11:03:11 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner onboarding: pilot intro + split/registration questions

Alyssa —

Appreciate the clear list. Below is a pragmatic set of guardrails we’ve been operating with that should map cleanly to your revops flow. Happy to iterate but this should unblock the pilot contract.

Partner registration and ownership
- Valid registration = partner registration form submission OR a documented demo intro that results in a logged HubSpot lead (we’ll accept either as provenance).
- Ownership rule = first-to-register wins. If multiple submissions exist, the earliest timestamped registration gets priority.
- Nurture and lookback windows = 90-day primary nurture from registration; 12-month claim window for prior contacts if partner provides CRM records showing prior engagement.

Anti-surfing / protection
- If a Redwood AE is already engaged (defined as active opportunity with a Redwood rep owner in our CRM), Redwood maintains the account for that open opportunity.
- If both Bridger and Redwood submit close-in-time registrations without an open opportunity, we mark it as a contested lead and trigger a quick review (30-day arbitration window).

Commission structure (pilot baseline)
- Intro-only (referral that results in paid revenue): 15% revenue share for first 12 months of subscription revenue.
- Technical integration (partner-led integration validated by Redwood): 20% for the first 12 months.
- Renewal cliff: commissions paid only on net new revenue; renewals beyond year one default to a 5% referral credit unless we co-sell.
- Clawback: refund/chargeback protection on cancellations within 60 days; see finance cadence below.

Operational items
- Payment cadence: payouts to partner are net-45 after Redwood receives payment from customer. Finance will require a partner W-9 / invoice cadence.
- Tracking: please register prospects in the partner portal: https://portal.redwood.com/partners/register?ref=bridger (this creates the HubSpot record automatically).

Next steps
1) I’ll circulate a short playbook and standard SOW template (draft attached).
2) If that looks good, legal can prepare a simple referral addendum + SOW that we can run through DocuSign.

Attaching the partner playbook for quick review.

—Logan
Head of Partnerships | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: partner-playbook-v1.pdf (pdf)]


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>, Alyssa Chen <alyssa@bridgersys.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 08:45:02 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner onboarding: pilot intro + split/registration questions

Looping in a couple of items from legal and finance so we don’t have surprises in the SOW: 

Legal notes (need to land in addendum)
- Clawback language: 60-day pro-rata refund window; for multi-year prepayments we require a defined waterfall for partner repayment (we’ll provide draft language).
- Evidence: partners must retain demonstrable provenance (form timestamps, signed intro emails) to assert ownership; we will accept CSV exports from partner CRM as supporting evidence.

Finance notes
- Payment schedule: net-45 after customer invoice is paid to Redwood. Commission remittance will be quarterly for pilots under $50k to reduce admin overhead.
- KYC/invoicing: Bridger needs to provide billing details (legal entity name, tax ID or W-9 equivalent).

Ops / tracking
- We created a stub HubSpot record for this flow: HUBS-2048123 — please use that as the canonical place while we wire partner portal integration.

I’ve attached a draft of our clawback terms so legal can comment inline. If everyone is aligned, I’ll ask contracts to spin a short referral addendum + pilot SOW and generate a DocuSign packet.

Thanks,
Marissa
Senior Partner Ops | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood-clawback-terms-draft.docx (docx)]

Hi Samantha / Tom —

Thanks again for the call today — appreciated the clarity on the ingestion volumes and the two pilot use-cases (search re-ranking + chat). Quick recap and proposed next steps so we’re aligned:

- Scope: 3-month pilot, up to 10M tokens/month, staging + prod endpoints, redacted PII only stored in KV cache for 24 hours.
- Deliverables: Pilot SOW draft (attached), integration checklist, and a one-hour enablement session.
- Success criteria: 90th pctile latency < 220ms, cost per 1k tokens under target, and NPS on response quality > 4/5 for sample prompts.

Next actions (I’ll own):

Best,
Logan Wright
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

title: Clarify: why some requests are temporary vs archived
preview:
From: Alana Baker <alana@brightbank.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.ai>
Cc: security@brightbank.com
Date: Sat, 15 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Clarification: temporary vs archived request logs

Hi Logan,

Thanks for the demo last week. Two follow-ups from our security team: can you clarify why some API requests show up as temporary traces while others end up archived in our session logs? The team is trying to map which artifacts are considered ephemeral (auto-purged) vs persistent (retained under default policy).

Specifically:
- Are streamed tokens ever persisted anywhere by default?
- Do intermediate KV/cache entries count as stored artifacts?
- What triggers a request to be treated as an \"archived\" record vs a transient one?

We need this to decide whether to request a retention addendum or rely on default controls. If you have a short example mapping and a clear list of steps for a customer-initiated purge, that would be ideal.

Appreciate a concise answer we can include in our internal memo.

Cheers,
Alana Baker
Security Program Manager
BrightBank

[attachment: scope-question.csv (CSV, 2KB)]

From: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.ai>
To: Alana Baker <alana@brightbank.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 17 Aug 2026 08:46:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarification: temporary vs archived request logs

Hi Alana — thanks for the clear questions. Short answer up front: streamed tokens are not retained by default as final artifacts; final response outputs and some derived artifacts can be retained per our default capture window. Below is the concise mapping and steps you requested.

Summary mapping (default behavior):
- Streamed tokens (partial, in-flight chunks): ephemeral by default. We do not index or store streaming token fragments as persistent records. They exist in-memory during the session and are discarded once the response completes.
- Final generated outputs (the assembled answer returned to the API client): may be stored under the default retention horizon for troubleshooting and quality (typical default: 45 days for hosted API). Customers can opt into shorter windows or request no retention.
- Prompts / request payloads: stored by default (same horizon) unless opt-out is configured at the project or org level.
- KV cache / runtime-only caches: treated as ephemeral; not persisted to the customer log store by default. Persistent key-value stores used by the customer (e.g., external DBs) are unaffected.
- Derivatives (embeddings, reranking indexes, fine-tune artifacts): treated according to the specific product flow and may be retained longer; these are called out explicitly in the console when created.

Why some items get archived vs transient: archival/persistence is decided by artifact type + feature path + tenant settings. For example, if a request triggers an evaluation job, the system will persist both request and response in the evaluation artifact store (longer horizon). Normal API calls without those flags follow the default capture horizon.

How to request immediate purge / addendum steps:
1) Open a support ticket (we can also do this on your behalf) — see HubSpot BR-9876 (link below).
2) Specify scope: session IDs, time ranges, or request IDs.
3) We validate the request, run an erasure job, and provide a confirmation report.
4) For contractual retention changes (longer/shorter than default), we issue a retention addendum (DocuSign link below).

I attached a short one-page summary and a policy excerpt for your legal team. If you want, Kimberly can join a 30-min call to walk security through a live example and the purge runbook.

Attachments: redwood-retention-summary.pdf (1 page), policy-excerpt.txt
Useful links: https://support.redwood.ai/article/retention-defaults | HubSpot BR-9876 | DocuSign retention addendum

Best,
Logan Wright
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
[attachment: redwood-retention-summary.pdf (PDF, 88KB)]

From: Alana Baker <alana@brightbank.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.ai>
Cc: security@brightbank.com
Date: Mon, 17 Aug 2026 10:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarification: temporary vs archived request logs

Thanks Logan — very helpful. Quick follow-up from our security lead: does the 'ephemeral by default' statement for streaming extend to partial responses when a client disconnects mid-stream (i.e., incomplete answers)? Also, will the purge report include hashes or IDs we can reconcile with our audit logs?

If you can confirm the disconnect scenario and the purge report fields, we'll scope the ticket.

-Alana

On Mon, Aug 17, 2026 at 08:46 AM Logan Wright <logan@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Hi Alana — thanks for the clear questions. Short answer up front: streamed tokens are not retained by default as final artifacts; final response outputs and some derived artifacts can be retained per our default capture window. Below is the concise mapping and steps you requested.
> 
> [snip summary mapping and steps]


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.ai>
To: Alana Baker <alana@brightbank.com>, Logan Wright <logan@redwood.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 17 Aug 2026 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarification: temporary vs archived request logs

Hi Alana — quick clarifications to ensure auditability:

- Disconnects mid-stream: incomplete/aborted streams are treated the same as in-flight chunks — they are not written to the persistent log store by default. If an application explicitly calls a \"finalize\" flag or the client SDK retries and completes the response, only the completed final output is eligible for retention.
- Purge report contents: our erasure confirmations include request IDs, request timestamps, and SHA256 hashes of the stored artifact where applicable so you can reconcile with your logs. We redact any content payload in the report, but the hash and IDs are available for audit purposes.

If you'd like, we can attach a small sample purge report (redacted) to the HubSpot ticket so BrightBank's auditors can validate the reconciliation workflow.

Logan — I'll coordinate a 30-minute slot tomorrow to run through the purge workflow demo if that works for you and Alana.

Thanks,
Kimberly Park
Head of Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference

[attachment: policy-excerpt.txt (TXT, 6KB)]

title: CI runner ephemeral credentials - remediation update & evidence ETA
preview:
From: Alex Torres <alex.torres@prismcloud.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.ai>; Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 15 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: [Action requested] CI runner ephemeral tokens leaking to public logs - ask for remediation status

Hi Logan / Monica,

Thanks for taking a look. Our security team surfaced a finding during a routine supply-chain review: one of the CI runners used to build your open-source connector appears to have emitted ephemeral credentials (short-lived tokens) into build logs that were publicly accessible for ~36 hours. We believe the tokens were scoped to a temporary service account used by the runner.

Can you confirm whether these tokens correspond to Redwood-managed runners, and provide:
- Confirmation of the root cause and whether it was a misconfigured third-party runner or our CI template
- Remediation actions already taken (rotations, config changes)
- ETA for producing an evidence bundle we can include in our audit (rotation logs, pipeline config diffs, mitigation checklist)

We're running a downstream assessment tied to our SOC 2 renewal and need evidence by Aug 21 to keep our deadline. If you can share interim proof (screencaps, rotation entries) sooner that would help.

Appreciate the quick turnaround — happy to set a short call.

Alex
Alex Torres
Lead DevSecOps, PrismCloud
[attachment: sample_log_snippet.txt]


From: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.ai>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 15 Aug 2026 10:00:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: [Action requested] CI runner ephemeral tokens leaking to public logs - ask for remediation status

Team — forwarding PrismCloud's ask. High level plan below; Monica please confirm timings and owners.

Summary of proposed actions (initial):
1) Identify scope: which runner IDs and which service-account tokens were involved (Monica)
2) Rotate any affected tokens and issue short incident note to downstream partners (Monica)
3) Patch CI templates to avoid printing secrets and set build log retention to private by default (Infra Eng)
4) Produce evidence bundle (rotation audit entries, pipeline config diffs, redacted log snaps) and upload to shared drive for PrismCloud. Aim for intermediate bundle by Tue 8/17, full evidence by Fri 8/20.

Attachments: ci-runner-rotation-checklist.md (initial checklist for rotation/verification).

Monica — can you own steps 1-2 and confirm whether we need infra help to change log retention? Karthik — I'll loop you into the customer-facing note once we have confirmation.

Thanks,
Logan Wright
Director, Security & Partnerships
Redwood Inference
[attachment: ci-runner-rotation-checklist.md]


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alex Torres <alex.torres@prismcloud.com>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 15:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: CI runner ephemeral tokens - remediation update

Logan / Karthik / Alex — quick progress update and evidence pointers.

What we found:
- A public-facing GitHub Actions runner (self-hosted) used a temporary service account token issued by our legacy CI onboarding script. The script printed a one-line debug variable (TOKEN=...) into the runner log for ~36 hours. This was a config mistake in the onboarding template (not an orchestration-level compromise).

Immediate remediation performed (completed 2026-08-16 11:46 PDT):
- Rotated all service-account keys that could have been issued by that template (rotation IDs: rot-20260816-001..004). Rotation entries are logged in our secrets-vault audit (see drive manifest).
- Updated the CI onboarding script to remove debug echo and to redact any environment variables printed to stdout.
- Updated GitHub repo-runner config to mark logs as private for all internal runners; added branch-protection on runner repo to restrict admin changes.
- Implemented a pipeline-level lint that fails the build if environment variables are printed to stdout.

Remaining items and ETAs:
- Evidence bundle (redacted logs + rotation audit + config diffs): ETA 2026-08-17 EOD (interim).
- Full remediation report mapping to SIG/CAIQ controls and SOC2 CC6.1: ETA 2026-08-20 COB.
- Optional: we can provide a short recorded walkthrough or ephemeral screen share on request (suggest Wed 8/18).

Attachments delivered: pt_report_excerpt.pdf (excerpt of penetration validation showing the log snippet redaction point), drive_evidence_manifest.txt (list of files in the shared evidence folder).

Notes for the customer: all rotated credentials were short-lived service-account keys with no lateral access to production; we have audited access logs and found no replay attempts. We'll include the audit entries in the evidence bundle.

Monica Patel
Senior Security Engineer
Redwood Inference

Rotation job rot-20260816-001 completed successfully. Audit ID: audit-7f9a2c.

Subject: Re: CI runner ephemeral credentials - remediation update & evidence ETA

Alex — thanks again for flagging this. Short summary for your audit team and next steps: 

1) Root cause: CI onboarding template emitted a debug line exposing ephemeral service-account tokens in runner logs.
2) Containment: affected tokens rotated (rot-20260816-001..004) and corresponding audit entries recorded. No evidence of token replay or access to production resources.
3) Remediation implemented: onboarding script patched, pipeline lint added, runner repo log retention set to private, branch protections enforced.
4) Evidence ETA: interim bundle (redacted logs + rotation audit + diffs) by 2026-08-17 EOD; comprehensive SOC2/SIG mapping and final evidence set by 2026-08-20 COB.

We attached the rotation checklist and a short penetration test excerpt for your triage. I've also uploaded the interim evidence to a shared drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-prism-evidence (manifest included).

If you'd like a recorded walkthrough, Monica can run through the pipeline diffs on Wed 8/18. Karthik — I'll let you handle the customer billing/contract note if PrismCloud needs an extension to their audit window.

Regards,
Logan Wright

Redwood Inference

POC goal (success criteria)
- Demonstrate <component>: end-to-end generation latency under 300ms for 8k context on sample dataset A.
- Integration: API-based inference integrated with your staging pipeline (2 endpoints: chat + embeddings) and weekly throughput of ~200k tokens.
- Quality: baseline BLEU-like sample pass rate >= 85% on the 50-sample prompt set.

Evaluation plan & timeline

Attachments: poc-credentialing-checklist.pdf (detailed steps), redwood-ssoconfig-template.xml (SAML template).

title: Urgent: TidalWave - spike in 5xx and token latency impacting inference
preview:
From: Alex Jensen <alex.jensen@tidalwave.ai>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: SRE Team <sre@tidalwave.ai>
Date: Sun, 9 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent

Hi Logan / Marissa,

We started seeing a sharp jump in 5xx errors and token latency around 08:45 UTC this morning on our production inference endpoints (model: rw-large-v1, region: us-west-2). Symptoms: p50 ~40ms (normal), p95 up to 1.2s, p99 up to 3.6s, and a 5xx rate rising from ~0.2% to 6% over the past 30 minutes. Our app logs show a lot of DNS timeouts followed by backend 502/504 responses.

What we’ve done so far:
- Rolled a small test client to a different VPC subnet — same behavior.
- Captured tcpdump during the window and attached a pcap.
- No config changes on our side since yesterday.

Attached: packet_capture_0809.pcap (pcap) and a short server log excerpt.

This is impacting our checkout flow — can we get immediate help? If this is on Redwood’s side, we need mitigation and an ETA for a full fix.

Thanks,
Alex Jensen
Head of Platform, TidalWave Labs
alex.jensen@tidalwave.ai
[Attachment: packet_capture_0809.pcap (pcap)]

From: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Jensen <alex.jensen@tidalwave.ai>, SRE Team <sre@tidalwave.ai>
Date: Sun, 9 Aug 2026 09:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent

Team — forwarding TidalWave’s report. Customer is seeing a sudden jump in 5xx and p99 token latency. They captured a pcap that suggests DNS timeouts and retries leading to backend errors. This is live production and impacting revenue-critical flow.

Immediate ask: 1) Can SRE/edge-engineering confirm whether our US peering/NATs show anomalies for TidalWave’s VPC ranges? 2) If it’s a DNS/peering handoff issue, what temporary routing changes or fallback variants can we enable to bring errors down quickly? 3) CS: prepare an exec update and credit proposal if we’re looking at an SLA miss.

I’m assigning ownership to Marcus for first investigation and Naomi for customer communications. Keep me in the loop — I’ll be pulling in Leadership if this expands.

— Logan
Logan Wright
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
logan.wright@redwood.com
[Quoted message follows]
> From: Alex Jensen <alex.jensen@tidalwave.ai>
> Date: Sun, 9 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
> Subject: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent
>

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Jensen <alex.jensen@tidalwave.ai>
Date: Sun, 9 Aug 2026 10:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent

Taking point on triage. Initial findings from a quick look:
- Between 08:40–09:00 UTC we see elevated TCP retransmits and repeated SYN timeouts from edges handling traffic between us-west-2 and TidalWave’s peered VPC prefixes (AS-path: peering -> customer-gateway -> nat-gw).
- DNS resolution for token-service.redwood.net shows intermittent NXDOMAIN/timeouts in the same window — appears correlated with the elevated retransmits.
- Kernel conntrack counters on the NAT gateway show spikes and rapid ephemeral port churn for the customer’s prefix, which is consistent with a burst of re-originated connections (likely healthcheck/keepalives).

Actionable mitigations I recommend now (can be applied within 10–15m):
1) Temporarily steer TidalWave’s traffic onto backup peering (us-west-1) for inference requests — we can do this per-prefix with route priority.
2) Increase DNS resolver retry for affected edge cluster to reduce application-visible DNS timeouts for 60m.
3) Disable the tenant-level proxy for their subnet (falls back to direct egress) to remove an extra hop that may be amplifying timeouts.

I’ve attached our support logs and a short grep of kernel counters. If routing change is OK, I’ll coordinate with Infra to apply and monitor.

Attachments: support_logs_0809.tar.gz

— Marcus
Marcus Lin
SRE Lead, Inference Platform
marcus.lin@redwood.com
[Attachment: support_logs_0809.tar.gz (tar.gz)]

From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 9 Aug 2026 11:20:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Fwd: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent

Summary for execs and next steps:
- Impact: TidalWave Labs (production) saw a 6% 5xx rate and p99 token latency spike to ~3.6s for ~45 minutes starting 08:45 UTC. Estimated request loss ~0.5% of daily revenue for affected window — awaiting final calc from Billing.
- Probable cause: transient peering/NAT churn combined with DNS resolution failures at the edge cluster serving the customer’s prefixes.
- Immediate mitigations performed / proposed:
  • Marcus to steer traffic to backup peering now (in progress).
  • Increase resolver retries for 1 hour (applied).
  • Disable tenant proxy for affected subnets as a temporary fallback (pending Marcus confirmation).
- Customer comms: Logan/Marissa to notify TidalWave that we’re actively mitigating and will provide a post-incident summary and credit proposal.
- Exec ask: If mitigation does not restore <0.5% 5xx within 60m, recommend opening an SLA credit package and scheduling an exec-level incident call. Approval requested to prepare up to a 10% month credit as a hold (finalized after root cause and impact calc).

I’ve created a diagnostics folder with packet captures and kernel counters: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/folders/abc123-tidalwave-diagnostics.

Naomi Feldman
Director, Customer Success
naomi.feldman@redwood.com
[Attachment: tidalwave_sla_brief.pdf (pdf)]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 9 Aug 2026 15:42:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Fwd: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent

Thanks — I’ll patch the customer update now and schedule the postmortem for tomorrow 10:00 PDT. I’ll include the proposed credit hold (up to 10%) and ask their execs for any additional impact data.

Logan: I’ll send the customer-facing note in 10 minutes and post the notes to the HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/deal/DW-4521.

— Marissa

> Subject: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent
>

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Jensen <alex.jensen@tidalwave.ai>
Date: Sun, 9 Aug 2026 10:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent

Taking point on triage. Initial findings from a quick look:
- Between 08:40–09:00 UTC we see elevated TCP retransmits and repeated SYN timeouts from edges handling traffic between us-west-2 and TidalWave’s peered VPC prefixes (AS-path: peering -> customer-gateway -> nat-gw).
- DNS resolution for token-service.redwood.net shows intermittent NXDOMAIN/timeouts in the same window — appears correlated with the elevated retransmits.
- Kernel conntrack counters on the NAT gateway show spikes and rapid ephemeral port churn for the customer’s prefix, which is consistent with a burst of re-originated connections (likely healthcheck/keepalives).

Actionable mitigations I recommend now (can be applied within 10–15m):
1) Temporarily steer TidalWave’s traffic onto backup peering (us-west-1) for inference requests — we can do this per-prefix with route priority.
2) Increase DNS resolver retry for affected edge cluster to reduce application-visible DNS timeouts for 60m.
3) Disable the tenant-level proxy for their subnet (falls back to direct egress) to remove an extra hop that may be amplifying timeouts.

I’ve attached our support logs and a short grep of kernel counters. If routing change is OK, I’ll coordinate with Infra to apply and monitor.

Attachments: support_logs_0809.tar.gz

— Marcus
Marcus Lin
SRE Lead, Inference Platform
marcus.lin@redwood.com
[Attachment: support_logs_0809.tar.gz (tar.gz)]

From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>

Subject: RE: Fwd: Spike in 5xx + token latency hitting inference — urgent

Summary for execs and next steps:
- Impact: TidalWave Labs (production) saw a 6% 5xx rate and p99 token latency spike to ~3.6s for ~45 minutes starting 08:45 UTC. Estimated request loss ~0.5% of daily revenue for affected window — awaiting final calc from Billing.
- Probable cause: transient peering/NAT churn combined with DNS resolution failures at the edge cluster serving the customer’s prefixes.
- Immediate mitigations performed / proposed:
  • Marcus to steer traffic to backup peering now (in progress).
  • Increase resolver retries for 1 hour (applied).
  • Disable tenant proxy for affected subnets as a temporary fallback (pending Marcus confirmation).
- Customer comms: Logan/Marissa to notify TidalWave that we’re actively mitigating and will provide a post-incident summary and credit proposal.
- Exec ask: If mitigation does not restore <0.5% 5xx within 60m, recommend opening an SLA credit package and scheduling an exec-level incident call. Approval requested to prepare up to a 10% month credit as a hold (finalized after root cause and impact calc).

I’ve created a diagnostics folder with packet captures and kernel counters: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/folders/abc123-tidalwave-diagnostics.
